q: What is the primary function of the cardiovascular system?
a: The transport of materials to and from all parts of the body.

q: Describe the function and provide examples of: Materials Entering the Body.
a: Oxygen enters from the lungs and is transported to all cells. Nutrients and water enter from the intestinal tract and are transported to all cells.

q: Describe the function and provide examples of: Materials Moved from Cell to Cell.
a: Wastes move to the liver for processing. Immune cells and antibodies are continuously available. Hormones move from endocrine cells to target cells. Stored nutrients move from the liver and adipose tissue to all cells.

q: Describe the function and provide examples of: Materials Leaving the Body.
a: Metabolic wastes move from all cells to the kidneys. Heat moves from all cells to the skin. Carbon dioxide moves from all cells to the lungs.

q: Trace the organization of the cardiovascular system, starting and ending in the aorta.
a: Aorta -> systemic arteries -> capillaries -> systemic veins -> venae cavae -> right atrium -> right ventricle -> pulmonary arteries -> lungs -> pulmonary veins -> left atrium -> left ventricle -> aorta.

q: Define Capillaries
a: The microscopic vessels where blood exchanges material with the interstitial fluid.

q: Define Arteries
a: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart.

q: Define Veins
a: Blood vessels that return blood to the heart.

q: Define Septum
a: The central wall that divides the heart into left and right halves, ensuring blood on one side does not mix with blood on the other.

q: Define Atria (Atrium)
a: The thinner-walled upper chambers of the heart that receive blood returning to the heart from blood vessels.

q: Define Ventricle
a: The thick muscular lower chambers of the heart that pump blood out into the blood vessels.

q: Define Pulmonary arteries
a: Vessels that direct deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.

q: Define Pulmonary veins
a: Vessels that return newly oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium.

q: Define Pulmonary circulation
a: The blood vessels spanning from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium.

q: Define Systemic circulation
a: The blood vessels carrying blood from the left side of the heart to the tissues and back to the right side of the heart.

q: Define Aorta
a: The primary artery of the systemic circulation that receives blood from the left ventricle and branches to supply the body.

q: Define Superior vena cava
a: The major vein that returns deoxygenated blood from the upper body to the right atrium.

q: Define Inferior vena cava
a: The major vein that returns deoxygenated blood from the lower body to the right atrium.

q: Trace the flow of blood within the pulmonary circulation.
a: Right ventricle -> pulmonary trunk and arteries -> lungs (where oxygenation occurs) -> pulmonary veins -> left atrium.

q: Trace the flow of blood within the systemic circulation.
a: Left ventricle -> aorta -> ascending/descending systemic arteries -> capillaries -> systemic veins -> superior and inferior venae cavae -> right atrium.

q: Describe the external anatomy of the heart (location, apex, and base).
a: Located in the center of the thoracic cavity on the ventral side. The apex is the pointed inferior end angling to the left, resting on the diaphragm. The base is the broader superior end just behind the sternum.

q: Define Pericardium
a: A tough membranous sac that encases the heart, containing a thin layer of clear fluid that lubricates the external surface as it beats.

q: Define Myocardium
a: The cardiac muscle that comprises the bulk of the heart.

q: Define Pulmonary trunk
a: The major blood vessel that emerges from the base of the heart, directing blood from the right ventricle to the pulmonary arteries and lungs.

q: Define Atrioventricular (AV) valves
a: The first set of one-way valves guarding the openings between each atrium and its ventricle, consisting of thin tissue flaps joined to a connective tissue ring.

q: Define Semilunar valves
a: The second set of one-way valves between the ventricles and the arteries (aorta and pulmonary trunk), featuring three cuplike leaflets that prevent backward flow.

q: Define Papillary muscles
a: Moundlike extensions of ventricular muscle that tether the chordae tendineae, providing stability to the AV valves so they do not prolapse into the atria.

q: Define Tricuspid valve
a: The right atrioventricular valve, consisting of three flaps, that separates the right atrium and the right ventricle.

q: Define Bicuspid (Mitral) valve
a: The left atrioventricular valve, consisting of two flaps, that separates the left atrium and the left ventricle.

q: Define Aortic valve
a: The semilunar valve located between the left ventricle and the aorta.

q: Define Pulmonary valve
a: The semilunar valve located between the right ventricle and the pulmonary trunk.

q: Define Coronary circulation
a: The heart's own special blood supply, consisting of blood vessels that encircle the heart near its base and feed the cardiac muscle.

q: Define Coronary arteries
a: Arteries originating at the root of the aorta that run across the surface of the heart in shallow grooves, supplying oxygenated blood to the myocardium.

q: Define Coronary veins
a: Veins that run in parallel with the coronary arteries, draining deoxygenated blood from the myocardium.

q: Define Coronary sinus
a: A large vessel on the posterior aspect of the heart where approximately 85% of venous blood from the cardiac veins empties before entering the right atrium.

q: Explain the importance of the valves of the heart.
a: They ensure blood flows in one direction only and prevent the backward flow of blood once it has been ejected from a chamber.

q: Describe Autorhythmic cells (pacemakers).
a: Specialized myocardial cells (about 1% of total) that generate action potentials spontaneously without innervation. They are smaller, contain few contractile fibers, and have no organized sarcomeres.

q: Describe Contractile cells.
a: Typical striated muscle cells that make up the bulk of the heart, organized into sarcomeres. They branch, have a single nucleus, and are connected end-to-end via intercalated disks.

q: Explain the key role of pacemaker cells in initiating action potentials without innervation.
a: They have an unstable membrane potential (pacemaker potential) that starts at -60 mV and slowly drifts upward. When it reaches threshold, it fires an action potential spontaneously.

q: Diagram and explain Step 1 of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle.
a: An action potential enters the contractile cell from an adjacent cell via gap junctions in the intercalated disks.

q: Diagram and explain Step 2 of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle.
a: Voltage-gated L-type calcium channels open in the cell membrane and t-tubules, allowing calcium to enter the cell down its electrochemical gradient.

q: Diagram and explain Step 3 of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle. (Define CICR)
a: Calcium entry opens ryanodine receptor (RyR) calcium release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum. This process is called Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR).

q: Diagram and explain Steps 4 and 5 of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle.
a: Stored calcium flows out of the sarcoplasmic reticulum creating a calcium spark. Multiple sparks sum together to create a massive calcium signal in the cytosol.

q: Diagram and explain Step 6 of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle.
a: Calcium ions bind to troponin, which initiates the cycle of crossbridge formation and filament movement, causing the cardiac muscle to contract.

q: Describe the first step in myocardial relaxation.
a: Relaxation occurs when calcium unbinds from troponin. Myosin releases actin, and the contractile filaments slide back to their relaxed position.

q: Describe the role of RyR channels in myocardial EC-coupling.
a: Ryanodine receptor (RyR) channels are located in the sarcoplasmic reticulum. They open in response to calcium entering the cell, releasing stored calcium into the cytosol.

q: Describe the role of the Na+-Ca2+ antiporter (NCX) in myocardial relaxation.
a: It removes calcium from the cell by transporting one calcium ion out against its gradient in exchange for three sodium ions entering the cell down their gradient.

q: Describe the role of the sodium-potassium pump (Na+-K+-ATPase) in myocardial relaxation.
a: It maintains the sodium gradient by pumping out the sodium ions that entered the cell during the NCX calcium exchange.

q: Describe how calcium is stored during myocardial relaxation.
a: Calcium is actively pumped back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum for storage via Ca2+-ATPase.

q: Describe Phase 4 (Resting membrane potential) of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: The cell is at a stable resting membrane potential of approximately -90 mV.

q: Describe Phase 0 (Depolarization) of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: A wave of depolarization enters via gap junctions. Voltage-gated sodium channels open, sodium rapidly enters, and the membrane potential reaches about +20 mV.

q: Describe Phase 1 (Initial repolarization) of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Sodium channels close. The cell begins to repolarize briefly as fast potassium channels open and potassium leaves the cell.

q: Describe Phase 2 (The plateau) of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Fast potassium channels close and voltage-gated calcium channels finally open. The combination of calcium influx and decreased potassium efflux flattens the action potential into a plateau.

q: Describe Phase 3 (Rapid repolarization) of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Calcium channels close and slow potassium channels open. Potassium exits rapidly, returning the cell to its resting potential.

q: Explain the refractory period in cardiac muscle.
a: The time following an action potential during which a normal stimulus cannot trigger a second action potential. In cardiac muscle, it lasts almost as long as the entire muscle contraction.

q: Explain the relationship of summation and tetanus in cardiac muscle.
a: Because of the long refractory period, no summation can occur. This prevents tetanus, which is essential because ventricles must relax completely between contractions to fill with blood.

q: Describe the pacemaker potential of cardiac autorhythmic cells.
a: An unstable membrane potential that never rests. It starts at about -60 mV and slowly drifts upward toward threshold.

q: Describe If channels (funny current) in autorhythmic cells.
a: Channels permeable to both potassium and sodium that open at -60 mV. They allow net sodium influx, which slowly depolarizes the cell toward threshold.

q: Define HCN channels
a: Hyperpolarization-Activated Cyclic Nucleotide-Gated channels. This is the family of ion channels that the If (funny) channels belong to.

q: What ion movement is responsible for the steep depolarization phase of the action potential in autorhythmic cells?
a: At threshold, voltage-gated calcium channels open and a rapid influx of calcium rushes into the cell.

q: What ion movement is responsible for the repolarization phase in autorhythmic cells?
a: Calcium channels close at the peak of the action potential, and slow potassium channels open, allowing potassium to efflux.

q: Compare the membrane potential of autorhythmic vs. contractile cells.
a: Contractile cells have a stable resting potential at -90 mV. Autorhythmic cells have an unstable pacemaker potential that usually starts at -60 mV.

q: Compare the rising phase (depolarization) of autorhythmic vs. contractile action potentials.
a: In contractile cells, the rising phase is caused by rapid sodium entry. In autorhythmic cells, the rising phase is caused by rapid calcium entry.

q: Compare the action potential duration and refractory period of autorhythmic vs. contractile cells.
a: Contractile action potentials have an extended duration (200+ msec) with a long refractory period. Autorhythmic potentials are variable (150+ msec) with no significant refractory period.

q: Describe the role of the sinoatrial node (SA node).
a: Autorhythmic cells located in the right atrium that serve as the main pacemaker of the heart, initiating the depolarization wave.

q: Describe the role of the internodal pathway.
a: A specialized conducting system of noncontractile autorhythmic fibers that rapidly connects electrical activity from the SA node to the AV node.

q: Describe the role of the atrioventricular node (AV node).
a: A group of autorhythmic cells near the floor of the right atrium that acts as the only electrical pathway from the atria to the ventricles.

q: Explain AV node delay.
a: The AV node slows down the transmission of action potentials slightly. This delay allows the atria to complete their contraction before ventricular contraction begins.

q: Describe the role of the atrioventricular bundle (AV bundle / bundle of His).
a: A pathway of specialized fibers located in the ventricular septum that receives the electrical signal from the AV node and passes it down into the ventricles.

q: Describe the bundle branches.
a: Divisions of the AV bundle (left and right) that continue downward through the ventricular septum toward the apex of the heart.

q: Describe the Purkinje fibers.
a: Specialized conducting cells that divide from the bundle branches at the apex and spread outward among the contractile cells, transmitting signals very rapidly upward.

q: Define diastole and systole.
a: Diastole is the time during which cardiac muscle relaxes. Systole is the time during which cardiac muscle contracts.

q: Explain the physical actions during Late Diastole (Phase 1).
a: Both atria and ventricles are relaxed. The AV valves are open, and blood flows passively by gravity from the atria into the ventricles.

q: Explain the physical actions during Atrial Systole (Phase 2).
a: The atria contract, forcing a small amount of additional blood into the ventricles. This accomplishes the last 20% of ventricular filling.

q: Define end-diastolic volume (EDV).
a: The maximum volume of blood that the ventricles contain during the cardiac cycle, occurring at the end of ventricular relaxation/filling.

q: Explain the physical actions during Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction (Phase 3).
a: Ventricles begin to contract, pushing the AV valves closed. Blood has nowhere to go because the semilunar valves are still closed. Pressure rises, but blood volume does not change.

q: What creates the first heart sound (S1)?
a: Vibrations following the closure of the AV valves as ventricular systole begins.

q: Explain the physical actions during Ventricular Ejection (Phase 4).
a: Ventricular pressure continues to rise and exceeds arterial pressure. The semilunar valves open, and blood is ejected from the ventricles into the arteries.

q: Define end-systolic volume (ESV).
a: The minimum volume of blood remaining in the ventricles at the end of a contraction (ventricular ejection).

q: Explain the physical actions during Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation (Phase 5).
a: Ventricles relax and pressure falls. Semilunar valves snap closed. The AV valves remain closed because ventricular pressure is still higher than atrial pressure. Volume is unchanged.

q: What creates the second heart sound (S2)?
a: Vibrations created by the closure of the semilunar valves as ventricular pressure drops below arterial pressure.

q: Describe the components of an ECG: Segments and Intervals.
a: Segments are sections of baseline between two waves. Intervals are combinations of waves and segments.

q: Describe the P wave and its relationship to the cardiac cycle.
a: The P wave represents atrial depolarization. It is followed by the mechanical event of atrial contraction (atrial systole).

q: Describe the P-R segment and its relationship to the cardiac cycle.
a: It represents conduction through the AV node and AV bundle (AV node delay). Mechanically, atrial contraction continues during this electrically quiet period.

q: Describe the QRS complex and its relationship to the cardiac cycle.
a: It represents the progressive wave of ventricular depolarization. It initiates the mechanical event of ventricular contraction.

q: Identify the individual parts of the QRS complex.
a: The Q wave is the beginning of ventricular depolarization, the R wave is the peak, and the S wave is the end of ventricular depolarization.

q: Where does atrial repolarization occur on an ECG?
a: It is not represented by a special wave; it is incorporated into and masked by the QRS complex.

q: Describe the T wave and its relationship to the cardiac cycle.
a: The T wave represents ventricular repolarization. It initiates the mechanical event of ventricular relaxation.

q: Describe the S-T segment and its relationship to the cardiac cycle.
a: It is the segment between the QRS complex and the T wave, representing the time when the ventricles are fully contracting (ventricular ejection).

q: Explain the general relationship between pressure changes and flow through heart valves.
a: Blood flows from higher pressure to lower pressure. A valve opens when the pressure behind it exceeds the pressure ahead of it, and closes when the pressure ahead exceeds the pressure behind.

q: In the Wiggers diagram, why does the mitral (AV) valve open to start filling?
a: Because isovolumic ventricular relaxation causes left ventricular pressure to fall below left atrial pressure.

q: In the Wiggers diagram, why does the aortic valve open?
a: Because isovolumic ventricular contraction causes left ventricular pressure to rapidly rise until it exceeds aortic pressure.

q: Explain the relationship between heart rate, cardiac output, and stroke volume.
a: Cardiac output is the total volume pumped per minute. It is calculated by multiplying heart rate (beats per minute) by stroke volume (volume pumped per beat).

q: Define Cardiac Output (CO).
a: The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle in a given period of time. Average resting value is about 5 L/min.

q: Define Stroke Volume (SV).
a: The amount of blood pumped by one ventricle during a single contraction.

q: Define Ejection Fraction.
a: The percentage of the end-diastolic volume (EDV) that is ejected with one contraction. It is calculated as Stroke Volume divided by EDV.

q: How do you calculate Stroke Volume?
a: Stroke Volume = End-Diastolic Volume (EDV) minus End-Systolic Volume (ESV).

q: How do you calculate Cardiac Output?
a: Cardiac Output = Heart Rate multiplied by Stroke Volume.

q: Explain the role of the parasympathetic division in controlling heart rate at the cellular level.
a: Acetylcholine binds to muscarinic receptors, increasing potassium efflux and decreasing calcium influx. This hyperpolarizes the autorhythmic cell and slows the rate of depolarization, decreasing heart rate.

q: Explain the role of the sympathetic division in controlling heart rate at the cellular level.
a: Catecholamines (epinephrine/norepinephrine) bind to beta-1 receptors and use cAMP to increase ion flow through If and calcium channels. This speeds up depolarization, increasing heart rate.

q: Explain the role of phospholamban in sympathetic control of the heart.
a: Phosphorylation of phospholamban enhances calcium-ATPase activity in the sarcoplasmic reticulum. This speeds up calcium removal (shortening contraction duration) and increases stored calcium (creating more forceful contractions).

q: END
a: END

q: Describe the pathways of blood within the body, including its relationship with heart anatomy and oxygenation/deoxygenation.
a: Oxygenated blood leaves the Left Heart, enters the Aorta/Systemic Arteries, travels to Arterioles and Capillaries (exchange occurs, becoming deoxygenated). It travels through Venules to Systemic Veins (Venae Cavae), entering the Right Heart. It pumps via the Pulmonary Artery to the Lungs (oxygenation), returning via the Pulmonary Vein to the Left Heart.

q: What are the five major types of blood vessels?
a: 1. Arteries 2. Arterioles 3. Capillaries 4. Venules 5. Veins

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Arteries.
a: Structure/Properties: Thick smooth muscle, large amounts of elastic and fibrous connective tissue. Stiff and springy walls. Function: Act as a pressure reservoir that maintains a continuous driving pressure for blood flow during ventricular relaxation through elastic recoil.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Arterioles.
a: Structure/Properties: Walls contain several layers of smooth muscle but minimal elastic or fibrous tissue. Function: Act as the site of variable resistance; they direct the distribution of blood flow to individual tissues by selectively constricting and dilating.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Capillaries.
a: Structure/Properties: Thinnest walls, lacking smooth muscle and elastic/fibrous tissue. Composed of a single layer of flattened endothelium supported on a basal lamina. Function: Primary site of exchange of materials between plasma, interstitial fluid, and cells.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Venules.
a: Structure/Properties: Smallest vessels downstream from capillaries; thin exchange epithelium, minimal connective tissue. Larger venules begin to have smooth muscle. Function: Distinguish themselves by a convergent pattern of flow, carrying blood from capillaries to veins.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Veins.
a: Structure/Properties: Thinner walls than arteries, with less elastic tissue. They expand easily when filling with blood. Function: Act as the volume reservoir of the circulatory system, holding more than half of the circulating blood, which can be sent to the arterial side if needed.

q: What is the specific Location and Function of vascular smooth muscle?
a: Location: Found in layers (circular or spiral) surrounding the endothelium in most blood vessels (especially arteries and arterioles). Function: Maintains muscle tone (partial contraction) and regulates blood vessel diameter via vasoconstriction and vasodilation.

q: What is the specific Location and Function of metarterioles?
a: Location: Vessels branching off arterioles into capillary beds. Function: Wall is only partially surrounded by smooth muscle; acts as a bypass channel directing blood straight into the venous circulation if precapillary sphincters are constricted.

q: What is the specific Location and Function of precapillary sphincters?
a: Location: Muscle rings located at the junction where metarterioles flow into true capillaries. Function: Can open or close off capillaries to locally regulate blood flow into capillary beds.

q: What is the specific Location and Function of pericytes?
a: Location: Closely associated with the outer layer of capillaries, forming a meshlike layer between endothelium and interstitial fluid. Function: Contractile cells that contribute to capillary 'tightness' (decrease leakiness) and secrete factors influencing capillary growth.

q: Discuss the role of smooth muscle in blood vessels.
a: Vascular smooth muscle maintains a state of partial contraction called muscle tone. By contracting (vasoconstriction), it narrows the vessel lumen, increasing resistance. By relaxing (vasodilation), it widens the lumen, decreasing resistance and altering blood flow.

q: Define vasoconstriction.
a: The narrowing of the diameter of the blood vessel lumen, caused by the contraction of vascular smooth muscle.

q: Define vasodilation.
a: The widening of the diameter of the blood vessel lumen, caused by the relaxation of vascular smooth muscle.

q: Define muscle tone as it relates to blood vessels.
a: A state of partial contraction maintained at all times by the vascular smooth muscle cells in most blood vessels.

q: Define volume reservoir.
a: A functional term for systemic veins. Because veins have thin, easily expandable walls, they hold more than half of the blood in the circulatory system, which can be redistributed to the arterial side if blood pressure falls.

q: Explain what creates blood pressure.
a: Blood pressure is created by the force of ventricular contraction. Ejected blood expands the elastic walls of the aorta and arteries, which store the pressure and release it through elastic recoil, propelling blood forward.

q: Explain how blood pressure changes as blood flows through the systemic circulation.
a: Blood pressure is highest in the arteries and decreases continuously as it flows through arterioles, capillaries, venules, and veins. This decrease occurs because energy is lost as friction (resistance to flow) from the vessel walls and between blood cells.

q: Define systolic pressure.
a: The highest pressure in the arteries, occurring during ventricular systole (contraction). The average high in the aorta is 120 mm Hg.

q: Define diastolic pressure.
a: The lowest pressure in the large arteries, occurring during ventricular diastole (relaxation). The pressure falls to a typical low of 80 mm Hg.

q: Define pulse (pressure wave).
a: The rapid pressure increase that occurs when the left ventricle pushes blood into the aorta, which travels through the arteries about 10 times faster than the blood itself.

q: Define pulse pressure.
a: A measure of the strength of the pressure wave. It is calculated by the formula: Systolic pressure minus diastolic pressure.

q: Define respiratory pump.
a: A mechanism that aids venous return. Changes in pressure in the thoracic cavity during breathing assist in drawing venous blood upward against gravity toward the heart.

q: Define skeletal muscle pump.
a: A mechanism that aids venous return. When skeletal muscles contract, they compress the veins, forcing blood upward past one-way valves toward the heart.

q: Explain the relationship between blood flow, pressure gradients, and the resistance of the system to flow.
a: Blood flow obeys fluid rules: Flow is directly proportional to the pressure gradient (delta P) between two points, and inversely proportional to the resistance (R) of the vessels. Formula: Flow is proportional to delta P / R.

q: Describe the Process of estimating blood pressure using sphygmomanometry.
a:
1. Inflate a cuff around the upper arm above systolic pressure (>120 mm Hg) to stop arterial flow. 
2. Gradually release cuff pressure. 
3. When cuff pressure falls below systolic, blood squeezes through, creating Korotkoff sounds (turbulent flow) = Systolic pressure. 
4. When sounds disappear (flow smooths out) = Diastolic pressure.

q: Define mean arterial pressure (MAP).
a: A single value representing the driving pressure for blood flow. It is closer to diastolic pressure because diastole lasts twice as long as systole. Formula: MAP = diastolic pressure + 1/3 (pulse pressure).

q: Define Korotkoff sound.
a: A thumping noise heard through a stethoscope during sphygmomanometry, caused by the pulsatile, turbulent flow of blood squeezing through a still-compressed artery.

q: Explain the contributions of cardiac output and peripheral resistance to blood pressure.
a: Mean arterial pressure is a balance between flow into the arteries (Cardiac Output) and flow out of the arteries, which is determined by the resistance of the arterioles (Peripheral Resistance). Formula: MAP is proportional to Cardiac Output x Peripheral Resistance.

q: Explain the multiple factors that affect blood pressure (Mean Arterial Blood Pressure).
a:
1. Blood volume (determined by fluid intake/loss). 
2. Effectiveness of heart pump/Cardiac Output (determined by heart rate & stroke volume). 
3. Resistance to blood flow (determined by arteriole diameter). 
4. Distribution of blood between arterial and venous vessels (determined by vein diameter).

q: List the three mechanisms controlling arteriolar resistance.
a:
1. Local control (matches tissue flow to metabolic needs).
2. Sympathetic reflexes mediated by CNS (maintains MAP and homeostatic distribution).
3. Hormonal control (influences blood pressure directly and by altering autonomic reflex control).

q: Describe the Hormonal and Neural control of blood vessel diameter regarding Norepinephrine (Receptor and Action).
a:
Type: Neurotransmitter (Neural Control). 
Source: Sympathetic neurons. 
Receptor: Alpha-receptors on vascular smooth muscle. 
Action: Causes vasoconstriction (maintains tonic control).

q: Describe the Hormonal and Neural control of blood vessel diameter regarding Epinephrine (Receptor and Action).
a:
Type: Neurohormone (Hormonal Control). 
Source: Adrenal medulla. 
Receptor: Beta-2-receptors (on heart, liver, skeletal muscle arterioles). 
Action: Causes vasodilation to enhance blood flow during fight-or-flight.

q: Describe the Hormonal and Neural control of blood vessel diameter regarding Acetylcholine (Receptor and Action).
a:
Type: Neurotransmitter (Neural Control). 
Source: Parasympathetic neurons. 
Receptor: Muscarinic receptors. 
Action: Causes vasodilation (indirectly through nitric oxide production) primarily in the erection reflex.

q: Describe the Hormonal control of blood vessel diameter regarding Angiotensin II.
a:
Type: Hormone. 
Source: Plasma hormone cascade. 
Action: A powerful vasoconstrictor that increases blood pressure and alters kidney excretion of ions and water.

q: Describe the Process of myogenic autoregulation.
a:
1. Increased BP stretches vascular smooth muscle cells. 
2. Mechanically gated channels open. 
3. Cation entry depolarizes the cell. 
4. Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open, Ca2+ binds calmodulin. 
5. Activates MLCK causing crossbridge activity. 
6. Arterioles constrict, increasing resistance and decreasing flow.

q: Define Active hyperemia (Process).
a: A locally mediated increase in blood flow that accompanies an increase in metabolic activity. As metabolism rises, O2 drops and CO2 rises, which act as paracrine signals to dilate arterioles, decreasing resistance and increasing flow to bring O2 and wash away wastes.

q: Define Reactive hyperemia (Process).
a: An increase in tissue blood flow following a period of low perfusion (occlusion). Occlusion causes hypoxia, triggering NO and vasodilator accumulation. When flow resumes, the accumulated vasodilators immediately trigger significant vasodilation.

q: Explain the multiple factors that affect peripheral blood flow.
a: Flow is directly proportional to the Pressure Gradient (MAP minus right atrial pressure) and inversely proportional to Resistance. Resistance increases with length and viscosity, but decreases exponentially as the radius increases (Flow is proportional to radius to the 4th power). Radius is determined by reflex and local control.

q: Define the Cardiovascular control center (CVCC) (Location and Function).
a:
Location: An integrating center network in the medulla oblongata. 
Function: Coordinates reflex control of blood pressure and tissue blood distribution. Its primary function is to ensure adequate blood flow to the brain and heart by maintaining sufficient mean arterial pressure.

q: Define Baroreceptors (Location and Function).
a:
Location: Stretch-sensitive mechanoreceptors in the walls of the carotid arteries and the aorta. 
Function: Tonically monitor arterial blood pressure. Firing rate increases when blood pressure stretches the membrane, and decreases when pressure falls.

q: Describe the Steps of the Baroreceptor Reflex Mechanism in response to INCREASED blood pressure.
a:
1. Stimulus: Increased BP. 
2. Sensor: Carotid/Aortic baroreceptors increase firing rate. 
3. Afferent: Sensory neurons fire to CVCC. 
4. Integrator: CVCC decreases sympathetic & increases parasympathetic output. 
5. Target: Vasodilation and decreased cardiac output. 
6. Response: BP decreases (negative feedback).

q: Describe the target and tissue response of the Baroreceptor reflex when Sympathetic output DECREASES.
a:
Target 1: Arteriolar smooth muscle (alpha-receptors) relaxes, causing Vasodilation and decreased peripheral resistance. 
Target 2: Ventricular myocardium (beta-1 receptors) decreases force of contraction, leading to decreased cardiac output.

q: Describe the target and tissue response of the Baroreceptor reflex when Parasympathetic output INCREASES.
a:
Target: The SA node (muscarinic receptors) is stimulated by more Acetylcholine. 
Tissue Response: Slows the heart rate, leading to a decreased cardiac output.

q: Describe the Steps of the Baroreceptor Reflex Mechanism in response to DECREASED blood pressure (Orthostatic Hypotension).
a:
1. Standing pools blood in legs, decreasing venous return/BP. 
2. Baroreceptors decrease firing rate. 
3. CVCC integrates signal. 
4. CVCC increases sympathetic output and decreases parasympathetic output. 
5. Cardiac output and peripheral resistance increase. 
6. Mean arterial pressure is restored.

q: END
a: END

q: What are the constituents of blood?
a: Plasma (the extensive fluid matrix) and Cellular Elements (cells and cell fragments suspended in the plasma).

q: What are the formed cellular elements of blood?
a: Red blood cells (erythrocytes), White blood cells (leukocytes), and Platelets (thrombocytes).

q: What is Plasma?
a: The fluid extracellular matrix of blood, composed of 92% water, 7% proteins, and 1% dissolved organic molecules, ions, trace elements, and gases.

q: What are the general functions of plasma proteins?
a: Blood clotting, defense against foreign invaders, carriers for steroid hormones/cholesterol/drugs/ions, acting as hormones or extracellular enzymes, and contributing to osmotic pressure.

q: What are Albumins and their functions?
a: The most prevalent plasma proteins (about 60% of total). They act as major contributors to plasma colloid osmotic pressure and act as carriers for various substances.

q: What are Globulins and their functions?
a: Plasma proteins that function as clotting factors, enzymes, antibodies (immunoglobulins for immune defense), and carriers for various substances.

q: What is Fibrinogen and its function?
a: A plasma protein essential to blood clotting; it forms the fibrin threads that create the stabilized mesh of a clot.

q: What is Transferrin and its function?
a: An iron-transporting plasma protein that binds ingested iron and transports it in the blood to the bone marrow.

q: What are Pluripotent hematopoietic stem cells?
a: A single precursor cell type found primarily in the bone marrow that has the remarkable ability to develop into many different blood cell types.

q: What are Lymphoid stem cells?
a: Descendants of pluripotent stem cells that differentiate into lymphocytes, the white blood cells responsible for specific immune responses.

q: What are Committed progenitor cells?
a: Cells descended from uncommitted stem cells that have narrowed their possible fates and are committed to developing into one or perhaps two specific blood cell types.

q: What is the Precursor of the erythroblast series?
a: Progenitor cells differentiate into large, nucleated erythroblasts, which mature by pinching off their nucleus to become reticulocytes, ending up as mature red blood cells.

q: What is the Megakaryocyte series?
a: Progenitor cells develop into huge polyploid cells called megakaryocytes. The outer edges of megakaryocytes fragment and break off, ending up as platelets.

q: What is the Granulocytic series?
a: Progenitor cells that end up producing granulocytes: neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, and tissue mast cells (tissue basophils).

q: What is the Mononuclear series?
a: Progenitor cells that end up producing monocytes, which are phagocytic cells that migrate into tissues and develop into tissue macrophages.

q: What role do Cytokines play in precursor cells?
a: Cytokines are peptides or proteins released from one cell that regulate the differentiation, growth signaling, and maturation of hematopoietic precursor cells.

q: What specific Cytokines regulate hematopoiesis?
a: Erythropoietin (EPO) for red blood cells, Thrombopoietin (TPO) for megakaryocytes/platelets, and Colony-stimulating factors (CSFs)/Interleukins for leukocytes.

q: What is the definition of Hematocrit?
a: The ratio of red blood cells to plasma, expressed as the percentage of total blood volume occupied by packed red blood cells. Normal is 40-54% for men, 37-47% for women.

q: How is red blood cell morphology used to diagnose Hereditary Spherocytosis?
a: The cells lose their flattened biconcave disk shape and become spherical, which causes them to rupture easily due to a defective cytoskeleton.

q: How is red blood cell morphology used to diagnose Sickle cell anemia?
a: Abnormal hemoglobin crystallizes when giving up oxygen, causing cells to pull into a rigid sickle or crescent moon shape that jams small blood vessels.

q: How is red blood cell morphology used to diagnose Iron-deficiency anemia?
a: The cells appear microcytic (abnormally small) and hypochromic (pale color) due to a lack of red hemoglobin content.

q: What is the structure of Hemoglobin?
a: A large, complex protein composed of four globular protein chains (usually two alpha and two beta chains), each wrapped around an iron-containing heme group.

q: What is the function of the heme portion of hemoglobin?
a: Each heme group contains a carbon-hydrogen-nitrogen porphyrin ring with a central iron atom that reversibly binds to and transports oxygen.

q: What is the function of the globin portion of hemoglobin?
a: The four globular protein chains provide the structural framework wrapped around the heme groups and also assist in the transport of carbon dioxide.

q: Trace the pathway of iron from diet ingestion to the bone marrow.
a: Iron is ingested in the diet, absorbed by active transport in the small intestine, and binds to the carrier protein transferrin, which transports it through the plasma to the bone marrow.

q: How is iron used in the assembly of RBCs?
a: The bone marrow takes up the iron and uses it to make the heme groups of hemoglobin, incorporating them into developing erythroblasts as part of RBC synthesis.

q: What happens during the breakdown of tired RBCs?
a: RBCs live about 120 days. The spleen destroys old, fragile RBCs and scavenges their components, converting the remnants of the heme groups into a colored pigment called bilirubin.

q: What is the pathway and fate of Bilirubin?
a: Bilirubin is carried by plasma albumin to the liver. The liver either stores or metabolizes it into bile salts (excreted in feces) or it is excreted in urine by the kidneys.

q: What is the definition of Anemia?
a: A condition in which the blood cannot transport enough oxygen to the tissues because the hemoglobin content or red blood cell count is too low.

q: What causes Anemia?
a: Accelerated cell loss (blood loss/hemorrhage or hemolytic anemias where cells rupture) or decreased cell production (defective synthesis from lack of iron/folic acid/B12, or lack of erythropoietin).

q: What is Hemostasis?
a: The physiological process of keeping blood within a damaged blood vessel; it plugs the hole while still maintaining blood flow through the vessel.

q: What is Vasoconstriction (Hemostasis Step 1)?
a: The immediate constriction of damaged vessels caused by paracrine molecules from the endothelium, decreasing blood flow and pressure within the vessel temporarily.

q: What is the Vascular plug (Hemostasis Step 2)?
a: Platelets adhere to exposed collagen, become activated to release cytokines, and aggregate (stick to one another) to form a loose, temporary platelet plug.

q: How is the platelet plug an example of positive feedback?
a: Activated platelets release factors like Platelet-activating factor (PAF), which attract and activate more platelets, rapidly growing the localized plug.

q: What is the coagulation pathway (Hemostasis Step 3)?
a: A cascade that converts a loose platelet plug into a clot by forming a stabilized fibrin protein mesh that seals the hole until tissues are repaired.

q: What is the Intrinsic pathway of coagulation?
a: Also called the contact activation pathway. It begins when damage to tissue exposes collagen, utilizing proteins already present in the plasma starting with factor XII.

q: What is the Extrinsic pathway of coagulation?
a: Also called the cell injury pathway. It starts when damaged tissues expose tissue factor (factor III), which then activates factor VII.

q: How do precursor proteins function in the coagulation cascade?
a: At each step, an enzyme converts an inactive precursor protein into an active enzyme. The common pathway converts prothrombin to thrombin, which then converts fibrinogen to a fibrin mesh.

q: How does the stabilized fibrin mesh augment the platelet plug?
a: Fibrin fibers weave through the loose platelet plug, trap red blood cells within their mesh, and are cross-linked by active factor XIII to form a stable clot.

q: What results from the absence of elements in the coagulation cascade?
a: The failure to form a stable fibrin clot, resulting in inappropriate and excessive bleeding, easy bruising, or potentially fatal hemorrhage (e.g., Hemophilia).

q: How is the clot ultimately dissolved by the fibrinolytic system?
a: As the vessel repairs, thrombin works with tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) to convert plasminogen into the enzyme plasmin, which breaks down the fibrin mesh (fibrinolysis).

q: What is the mechanism of anticoagulation via platelet inhibition?
a: Intact, normal vascular endothelial cells convert their membrane lipids into prostacyclin and release nitric oxide (NO), which block platelet adhesion and aggregation.

q: What is the mechanism of anticoagulation via coagulation cascade inhibition?
a: Endothelial cells release anticoagulants like Heparin/Antithrombin III (blocks factors IX, X, XI, XII) and Protein C (inhibits factors V, VIII) to prevent fibrin production.

q: END
a: END

q: Describe the four major functions of the respiratory system.
a: 1. Exchange of gases between the atmosphere and the blood. 2. Homeostatic regulation of body pH. 3. Protection from inhaled pathogens and irritating substances. 4. Vocalization.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the conducting system (airways).
a: Passageways that lead from the external environment to the exchange surface of the lungs. They function to filter out foreign material, warm air to body temperature, and humidify air to 100 percent humidity.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the alveoli.
a: A series of interconnected sacs and their associated pulmonary capillaries. They form the primary exchange surface where oxygen moves into the blood and carbon dioxide moves into the air.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the upper respiratory tract.
a: Consists of the mouth, nasal cavity, pharynx, and larynx. Functions primarily to filter, warm, and humidify inhaled air.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the lower respiratory tract.
a: Also known as the thoracic portion. Includes the trachea, two primary bronchi, their branches, and the lungs.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the diaphragm.
a: A dome-shaped sheet of skeletal muscle that forms the floor of the thoracic cage. It is the primary muscle of quiet inspiration, dropping down toward the abdomen (flattening) when contracted.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the intercostal muscles.
a: Two sets of skeletal muscles (internal and external) that connect the 12 pairs of ribs. External intercostals pull ribs upward/outward during inspiration; internal intercostals pull ribs inward during active expiration.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the pleural sacs.
a: A double-walled sac that surrounds each lung. The inner visceral membrane covers the lung surface, and the outer parietal membrane lines the inside of the thorax.

q: Define: Pleura.
a: Pleural membranes that contain several layers of elastic connective tissue and numerous capillaries, forming the double-walled sac around each lung.

q: Describe the function of pleural fluid.
a: 1. Creates a moist, slippery surface so opposing membranes can slide across one another. 2. Holds the lungs tight against the thoracic wall via a fluid bond so the lungs move with the thoracic cage.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the pharynx.
a: The throat. A common passageway for food, liquids, and air located after the mouth and nasal cavity.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the larynx.
a: Located past the pharynx, it leads into the trachea. Contains the vocal cords, which are connective tissue bands that vibrate to create sound for vocalization.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the trachea.
a: The windpipe. A semiflexible tube extending down into the thorax held open by 15 to 20 C-shaped cartilage rings. It branches into the primary bronchi.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the primary bronchi.
a: A pair of semirigid tubes supported by cartilage that branch from the trachea. One bronchus goes to each lung.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of the bronchioles.
a: Small, collapsible passageways with walls of smooth muscle (lacking cartilage) that form from branched bronchi. They control airway diameter and air flow resistance.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of Type I alveolar cells.
a: Very thin epithelial cells that make up about 95 percent of the alveolar surface area. Their thinness allows gases to diffuse rapidly through them for gas exchange.

q: Describe the anatomy and function of Type II alveolar cells.
a: Smaller but thicker epithelial cells in the alveoli. They synthesize and secrete surfactant and transport solutes out of the alveolar air space to minimize fluid.

q: Define: Surfactant.
a: A mixture of proteins and phospholipids secreted by Type II alveolar cells that mixes with the thin fluid lining the alveoli to decrease surface tension and ease lung expansion.

q: Define the bulk flow of air.
a: The movement of a compressible mixture of gases directed from regions of higher pressure to regions of lower pressure.

q: Define the three principles of the bulk flow of air.
a: 1. Flow takes place from regions of higher pressure to lower pressure. 2. A muscular pump creates pressure gradients. 3. Resistance to air flow is influenced primarily by the diameter of the tubes.

q: How does the anatomy of the lungs relate to the three principles of bulk flow?
a: The skeletal muscles of the thorax act as the pump to create pressure gradients. The diameter of the conducting airways controls the resistance. This allows air to flow from higher to lower pressure.

q: Describe the mechanics of breathing.
a: The collective mechanisms by which ventilation takes place. It uses alternating contraction and relaxation of the thoracic muscles to change chest cavity volume, driving air in and out.

q: Define: Ventilation.
a: The exchange of air between the atmosphere and the lungs (breathing).

q: Define: Inspiration.
a: The movement of air into the lungs (inhalation).

q: Define: Expiration.
a: The movement of air out of the lungs (exhalation).

q: Explain the relationship of cardiac output and blood flow through the lungs.
a: The lungs receive the entire cardiac output of the right ventricle (5 L/min). As much blood flows through the lungs in 1 minute as flows through the rest of the body in the same time.

q: Explain the relationship between atmospheric pressure and partial pressure.
a: Atmospheric pressure is the total pressure exerted by the gas mixture. Partial pressure is the specific portion of that total pressure contributed by a single individual gas in the mixture.

q: Express the relationship between atmospheric pressure and partial pressure mathematically using Dalton's Law (Dry air).
a: Partial pressure of a gas = Atmospheric Pressure times the Percentage of the gas in the atmosphere.

q: Express the relationship between atmospheric pressure and partial pressure mathematically using Dalton's Law (Humid air).
a: Partial pressure of a gas = (Atmospheric Pressure minus Water Vapor Pressure) times the Percentage of the gas.

q: Explain the relationship between the pressure of a gas and the volume in which it is contained using Boyle's Law.
a: Pressure and volume have an inverse relationship. If the volume of a container of gas increases, the pressure of the gas decreases, and vice versa.

q: Understand Table 17.1: State Gas Law Principle 1 (Total pressure).
a: The total pressure of a mixture of gases is the sum of the pressures of the individual gases (Dalton's law).

q: Understand Table 17.1: State Gas Law Principle 2 (Dry air partial pressure).
a: To find partial pressure of a gas in dry air, multiply the gas's contribution percentage times atmospheric pressure.

q: Understand Table 17.1: State Gas Law Principle 3 (Humid air partial pressure).
a: To calculate partial pressure of a gas in humid air, subtract water vapor pressure from total pressure first, then multiply by the gas's contribution percentage.

q: Understand Table 17.1: State Gas Law Principle 4 (Gas movement).
a: Gases, singly or in a mixture, move from areas of higher pressure to areas of lower pressure.

q: Understand Table 17.1: State Gas Law Principle 5 (Boyle's Law).
a: If the volume of a container of gas changes, the pressure of the gas changes in an inverse manner.

q: Describe lung volumes and their relationship: Define Tidal volume.
a: The volume of air that moves during a single inspiration or expiration. Average during quiet breathing is roughly 500 mL.

q: Describe lung volumes and their relationship: Define Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV).
a: The additional volume of air inspired above the tidal volume at maximum effort.

q: Describe lung volumes and their relationship: Define Expiratory reserve volume (ERV).
a: The amount of air forcefully exhaled after the end of a normal passive expiration.

q: Describe lung volumes and their relationship: Define Residual volume (RV).
a: The volume of air remaining in the respiratory system after maximal exhalation. It cannot be measured directly.

q: Describe lung capacities and their relationship: Define Vital capacity (VC).
a: The maximum amount of air that can be voluntarily moved into or out of the respiratory system with one breath. Sum of Tidal Volume + Inspiratory Reserve Volume + Expiratory Reserve Volume.

q: Describe lung capacities and their relationship: Define Total lung capacity (TLC).
a: The absolute total volume of the lungs. Sum of Vital capacity + Residual volume.

q: Describe lung capacities and their relationship: Define Inspiratory capacity.
a: The sum of the tidal volume and the inspiratory reserve volume.

q: Describe lung capacities and their relationship: Define Functional residual capacity.
a: The sum of the expiratory reserve volume and the residual volume.

q: Explain how pressures and lung volumes change during normal inspiration.
a: Thoracic volume increases as muscles contract. This decreases alveolar pressure below atmospheric pressure, creating a gradient that pulls air into the lungs.

q: Explain how pressures and lung volumes change during normal expiration.
a: Thoracic volume decreases due to elastic recoil as muscles relax. This increases alveolar pressure above atmospheric pressure, pushing air out.

q: Diagram the alveolar pressure changes that occur during inspiration and expiration.
a: Starts at 0 mm Hg. Drops to -1 mm Hg mid-inspiration. Returns to 0 mm Hg at end inspiration. Rises to +1 mm Hg mid-expiration. Returns to 0 mm Hg at end expiration.

q: Diagram the intrapleural pressure changes that occur during inspiration and expiration.
a: Always subatmospheric. Starts at -3 mm Hg between breaths. Falls further to -6 mm Hg at the end of inspiration. Returns to -3 mm Hg at the end of expiration.

q: Compare and contrast compliance and elastance in respiratory physiology.
a: Compliance is the ability of the lung to stretch or expand easily. Elastance is the ability of the lung to resist being deformed and its ability to recoil to resting volume. They are reciprocals.

q: Explain the role of surfactants in respiratory physiology.
a: Surfactants decrease surface tension in the fluid lining the alveoli. This reduces the work required for breathing, prevents smaller alveoli from collapsing, and equalizes pressure among alveoli of different sizes.

q: Map the factors affecting airway resistance regarding Length and Viscosity.
a: Length of the system is constant and not a factor. Viscosity of air is usually constant, though humidity and altitude can alter it slightly.

q: Map the factors affecting airway resistance regarding the Upper airways.
a: Account for 90 percent of resistance. Diameter is affected by physical obstruction and mediated by mucus and other factors.

q: Map the local and reflex control mechanisms involved with bronchoconstriction.
a: Parasympathetic neurons (via muscarinic receptors), histamine, and leukotrienes cause bronchoconstriction. Decreased carbon dioxide levels also trigger constriction locally.

q: Map the local and reflex control mechanisms involved with bronchodilation.
a: Increased carbon dioxide in exhaled air causes local bronchodilation. Circulating epinephrine acting on Beta-2 receptors also causes bronchodilation.

q: Compare and contrast total pulmonary ventilation and alveolar ventilation.
a: Total pulmonary ventilation measures all air moved in/out per minute. Alveolar ventilation measures only the fresh air reaching the alveoli, factoring out dead space, making it a better measure of gas exchange efficiency.

q: Define: Total pulmonary ventilation.
a: The volume of air moved into and out of the lungs each minute. Calculated as: ventilation rate times tidal volume.

q: Define: Alveolar ventilation.
a: The volume of fresh air that reaches the alveoli each minute. Calculated as: ventilation rate times (tidal volume minus dead space volume).

q: Define: Anatomic dead space.
a: The volume of air in the conducting airways (like the trachea and bronchi) that does not reach the alveoli and therefore does not participate in gas exchange.

q: Define: Maximum voluntary ventilation.
a: The maximum amount of air a person can move into and out of the lungs when breathing as deeply and quickly as possible (up to 170 L/min).

q: Explain the local control mechanism by which ventilation and alveolar flow are matched at the bronchioles.
a: Bronchiolar diameter is regulated primarily by carbon dioxide levels. An increase in carbon dioxide in expired air causes bronchioles to dilate; a decrease causes them to constrict.

q: Explain the local control mechanism by which ventilation and alveolar flow are matched at the pulmonary arterioles.
a: Arteriole diameter is regulated by oxygen levels. A decrease in oxygen around the alveoli causes pulmonary arterioles to constrict, diverting blood to better-ventilated areas.

q: Review Question 1: List four functions of the respiratory system.
a: 1. Gas exchange between atmosphere and blood. 2. Homeostatic regulation of body pH. 3. Protection from inhaled pathogens and irritating substances. 4. Vocalization.

q: Review Question 5: Name the anatomical structures that an oxygen molecule passes on its way from the atmosphere to the blood.
a: Nasal cavity/mouth -> pharynx -> larynx -> trachea -> primary bronchi -> smaller bronchi -> bronchioles -> respiratory bronchioles -> alveolar air space -> across alveolar epithelium -> basement membrane -> capillary endothelium -> plasma.

q: Review Question 10: Describe the changes in alveolar and intrapleural pressure during one respiratory cycle.
a: Alveolar pressure starts at 0, drops to -1 (inspiration), returns to 0, rises to +1 (expiration), and returns to 0. Intrapleural stays negative: starts at -3, drops to -6 (end of inspiration), and returns to -3.

q: Review Question 11: How do you calculate ventilation rate from a 15-second spirogram tracing?
a: Count the number of respiratory cycles that occur in the 15-second interval and multiply by 4 to get breaths per minute.

q: Review Question 13: Match the following items with their correct effect on the bronchioles: histamine, epinephrine, acetylcholine, increased carbon dioxide.
a: Histamine = bronchoconstriction. Epinephrine = bronchodilation. Acetylcholine = bronchoconstriction. Increased carbon dioxide = bronchodilation.

q: Review Question 14: What is the function of surfactants in general? In the respiratory system?
a: General: Disrupt cohesive forces between water molecules to reduce surface tension. Respiratory: Decreases alveolar surface tension, reducing lung stretch resistance and preventing small alveoli collapse.

q: Review Question 15: If a person increases her tidal volume, what would happen to her alveolar partial pressure of oxygen?
a: Alveolar partial pressure of oxygen would increase because more fresh air (with a higher partial pressure of oxygen) reaches the alveoli with each breath.

q: Review Question 16: Compare and contrast Compliance and elastance.
a: Compliance is the ease with which lungs stretch (high compliance = stretches easily). Elastance is the ability to resist deformation and return to resting shape. They are reciprocals of each other.

q: Review Question 16: Compare and contrast Inspiration, expiration, and ventilation.
a: Inspiration is air entering lungs (active). Expiration is air leaving lungs (usually passive). Ventilation is the combined complete process of breathing.

q: Review Question 16: Compare and contrast Intrapleural pressure and alveolar pressure.
a: Intrapleural pressure is always subatmospheric in a sealed cavity. Alveolar pressure fluctuates slightly above and below atmospheric pressure, equalizing between breaths.

q: Review Question 16: Compare and contrast Total pulmonary ventilation and alveolar ventilation.
a: Total = rate times tidal volume. Alveolar = rate times (tidal volume minus dead space). Alveolar ventilation accounts for dead space and is a better gas exchange indicator.

q: Review Question 16: Compare and contrast Type I and type II alveolar cells.
a: Type I cells are thin, make up 95 percent of surface area, and are used for gas exchange. Type II cells are smaller/thicker and secrete surfactant.

q: Review Question 16: Compare and contrast Pulmonary circulation and systemic circulation.
a: Pulmonary circulation operates at low pressure (25/8 mm Hg) and low resistance to oxygenate blood. Systemic circulation operates at high pressure (120/80 mm Hg) to distribute oxygenated blood.

q: Review Question 17: List the major paracrines and neurotransmitters that cause bronchoconstriction. What receptors do they act through?
a: Histamine (H1 receptors); Acetylcholine (muscarinic receptors); Leukotrienes (leukotriene receptors); Substance P.

q: Review Question 17: List the major paracrines and neurotransmitters that cause bronchodilation. What receptors do they act through?
a: Carbon dioxide (directly relaxes smooth muscle); Epinephrine (circulating hormone acting on Beta-2 adrenergic receptors).

q: Review Question 18: What is the sequence of events during forced breathing (active expiration)?
a: Expiratory muscles (internal intercostals, abdominal muscles) contract, decreasing thoracic volume further, causing alveolar pressure to rise higher and air to flow out forcefully.

q: Review Question 19: Will airway resistance increase, decrease, or not change with bronchodilation?
a: Decrease.

q: Review Question 19: Will intrapleural pressure increase, decrease, or not change during inspiration?
a: Decreases (becomes more negative).

q: Review Question 19: Will air flow increase, decrease, or not change with bronchoconstriction?
a: Decrease.

q: Review Question 19: Will bronchiolar diameter increase, decrease, or not change with increased carbon dioxide partial pressure?
a: Increases (bronchodilation).

q: Review Question 19: Will tidal volume increase, decrease, or not change with decreased compliance?
a: Decreases (because the lungs are harder to stretch).

q: Review Question 19: Will alveolar pressure increase, decrease, or not change during expiration?
a: Increases (rises above atmospheric pressure).

q: Review Question 22: Which phase of ventilation usually depends on elastic recoil?
a: Expiration.

q: Review Question 23: Describe the partial pressure of oxygen of air in the primary bronchi during one respiratory cycle.
a: During inspiration, it rises as fresh air (about 150 mm Hg) enters. During expiration, it falls as stale alveolar air (about 100 mm Hg) passes through.

q: Review Question 28: Alveolar air partial pressure of oxygen is 100 mm Hg, but expired air is 120 mm Hg. Why is expired air higher?
a: Expired air is a mixture of alveolar air (100 mm Hg) AND dead space air (the last fresh air inspired, 150 mm Hg). This dead space air raises the average.

q: Review Question 30: A container has a volume of 500 mL and pressure of 60 mm Hg. If pressure becomes 150 mm Hg, what is the new volume?
a: Using Boyle's law: 60 times 500 = 150 times Volume 2. Volume 2 = 30000 / 150 = 200 mL.

q: Review Question 31: Calculate partial pressure of 21 percent oxygen in dry air at an atmospheric pressure of 760 mm Hg.
Review Question 32: Tidal volume = 400 mL, rate = 12 breaths/min, dead space = 120 mL. What is total pulmonary ventilation?
a: 12 times 400 = 4800 mL/min.

q: Review Question 32: Tidal volume = 400 mL, rate = 12 breaths/min, dead space = 120 mL. What is alveolar ventilation?
Review Question 33: Minute volume = 5004 mL/min, rate = 12 breaths/min, Vital capacity = 4800 mL, Expiratory Reserve Volume = 1000 mL. What is Inspiratory Reserve Volume?
a: Tidal volume = 5004 / 12 = 417 mL. Inspiratory capacity = 4800 - 1000 = 3800 mL. Inspiratory Reserve Volume = 3800 - 417 = 3383 mL.

q: Concept Check 7: What happens to net capillary filtration if left ventricular failure doubles pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure?
a: Net filtration out of capillaries increases, which can lead to pulmonary edema.

q: Concept Check 9: What is the partial pressure of nitrogen in a gas mixture containing 78 percent nitrogen at an atmospheric pressure of 720 mm Hg?
Concept Check 10: Calculate the partial pressure of oxygen of fully humidified air at atmospheric pressure 700 mm Hg with water vapor pressure 47 mm Hg (21 percent oxygen).
Concept Check 11: Are lung capacities a single volume or combinations of volumes?
a: Lung capacities are sums of two or more lung volumes.

q: Concept Check 12: Which lung volume cannot be measured directly by spirometry?
a: Residual volume.

q: Concept Check 13: If vital capacity decreases with age but total lung capacity does not change, what must be happening to residual volume?
a: Residual volume must be increasing, because Total Lung Capacity = Vital Capacity + Residual Volume.

q: Concept Check 14: As inhaled air becomes humidified, water vapor pressure increases. What happens to the partial pressure of oxygen?
a: It decreases because the water vapor dilutes oxygen's contribution to total pressure.

q: Concept Check 19: During hiccups (spastic diaphragm contraction), what happens to intrapleural and alveolar pressures?
a: Intrapleural pressure becomes more negative, causing alveolar pressure to drop below atmospheric pressure, so air rushes into lungs momentarily.

q: Concept Check 22: In coal miners with scarlike tissue covering alveolar surface area, what happens to lung compliance?
a: Lung compliance decreases, meaning the lungs become stiffer and harder to stretch.

q: Concept Check 23: Without surfactant, what happens to surface tension and the work of breathing?
a: Surface tension increases, causing the work required for breathing to increase greatly.

q: Concept Check 24: If a cancerous tumor narrows bronchiole lumens, what happens to airway resistance?
a: Resistance to air flow in those bronchioles increases.

q: Concept Check 25: What is the neurotransmitter and receptor for parasympathetic bronchoconstriction?
a: Neurotransmitter = acetylcholine. Receptor = muscarinic.

q: Concept Check 26: If a lung tumor decreases blood flow in one small section, what happens to partial pressures of oxygen and carbon dioxide in those alveoli?
a: Oxygen increases (no blood consuming it) and carbon dioxide decreases (no blood bringing it). Bronchioles compensate by constricting due to decreased carbon dioxide.
